items.title: Roots-aware MCP filesystem server pattern
items.why: The reference MCP servers now give you a copyable pattern for safe local tool access: explicit allowed directories, runtime roots updates, and client configs for Claude Desktop or VS Code.
how_to_steps: Start with the reference server registry, then choose the filesystem server when you need tightly scoped file access instead of a broad shell integration.
Configure the server in your MCP client with `npx -y @modelcontextprotocol/server-filesystem` or Docker and pass only the directories the agent should touch.
Prefer clients that support Roots so the allowed directories can update at runtime instead of hardcoding every path up front.
If the server fails under NVM or similar PATH managers, switch to an absolute `node` or `npx` path or a wrapper script before debugging higher-level agent behavior.
items.evidence: Primary docs include concrete `npx`, Docker, VS Code, and Claude Desktop configs plus Roots-based runtime directory updates; the repo is the official MCP reference server collection.
items.try_monday: Add one filesystem MCP server to a sandbox repo and restrict it to a single project folder before expanding access.

items.title: Scheduled Claude Code workflows with repo-scoped subagents
items.why: Claude Code's GitHub Action now supports comment-triggered and scheduled automation, while still following `CLAUDE.md` and custom agents in the repo.
how_to_steps: Run `/install-github-app` for the fast path or manually install the Claude GitHub App, add `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY`, and copy a workflow into `.github/workflows/`.
Start with `anthropics/claude-code-action@v1` and use `prompt` for the task you want automated.
Move project rules into `CLAUDE.md` so the action follows the same standards your local sessions use.
For scheduled or specialized jobs, store a custom agent in `.claude/agents/` and pass `claude_args: --agent <name>` or call it explicitly from the workflow prompt.
items.evidence: Official docs provide quick setup, manual install, cron examples, and v1 workflow syntax; the GitHub discussion shows the `--agent` pattern for scheduled custom subagents.
items.try_monday: Schedule one daily maintenance workflow that reads a repo-scoped agent and summarizes stale issues or flaky tests.

items.title: Codex CLI review-before-push workflow
items.why: Codex now bundles install, local agent mode, a separate review agent, subagents, MCP, and cloud tasks into one terminal workflow instead of separate tools.
how_to_steps: Install Codex with the standalone installer, npm, or Homebrew and run `codex` in the project you want it to work in.
Sign in, point Codex at the repo, and keep Git checkpoints before and after each task so you can revert cleanly.
Use the built-in local code review pass before you commit or push, rather than treating generation and review as the same step.
Add subagents, MCP servers, or Codex Cloud tasks only after the core local workflow is stable, so you do not debug orchestration and base setup at the same time.
items.evidence: OpenAI describes the CLI as open source and built in Rust for speed and efficiency, and documents separate review, subagents, MCP, and cloud-task flows in the same tool.
items.try_monday: Adopt a simple rule for one repo: every Codex-generated diff gets a separate Codex review pass before push.
